fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get this route protection wrapper thing finished up. Basically the core problem is that right now anyone can just navigate directly to protected pages without being logged in, and we're getting complaints that users sometimes see a flash of protected content before being kicked out, or worse they just stay on the page in a broken state. We want something reusable so devs don't have to copy-paste the same auth check into every single screen component.

The wrapper should handle the usual stuff — send people to login if they're not authenticated, and make sure the URL they were trying to get to is preserved somewhere in the redirect so we can send them back after they log in. Similar to what we did with the compatibility logic on the login module last time, there's also a pending/loading state to think about — we don't want to redirect someone mid-authentication.

Also we need it to support more advanced access rules beyond just 'is logged in', like checking profile fields. And it needs to work for nested routes too, not just top-level ones. There's also a case where we explicitly do NOT want to record the attempted URL — some routes are sensitive and we don't want that leaking into query params. The wrapper should also pass through any props or metadata the underlying component needs. Let's make sure this doesn't fire duplicate redirects after mount either, that was a problem before.

trigger_keywords: where is the attempted URL stored
how is the return location encoded
what query parameter holds the redirect path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The attempted URL is URL-encoded and stored in a query parameter named `redirect` on the failure destination. For example, navigating to `/auth?test=foo` while unauthenticated redirects to `/login?redirect=%2Fauth%3Ftest%3Dfoo`. Both the path and the original query string are included in the encoded value.

trigger_keywords: how to disable the redirect back
suppress return location
hide the attempted URL from the query string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There is a configuration option to suppress the return-location query parameter entirely. When this option is enabled, a denied navigation redirects to the failure destination with an empty search string — no `redirect` query parameter is appended at all. See the `no_redirect_back` policy in the test cases (feature5_redirect_back_options.json).

trigger_keywords: pending authentication state
loading while auth is in progress
what renders during authentication check
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When authentication is pending (in-progress), the guard must NOT redirect. The router path stays on the attempted protected route with an empty search string. If a custom loading component is configured it is rendered; if none is configured, an empty placeholder component is rendered and it receives no props (reported as `placeholder_props={}` in the test output). See feature2_pending_authentication.json.

trigger_keywords: profile-based access rules
custom authorization predicate
checking fields beyond just logged in
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Authorization rules can inspect arbitrary fields from the selected session profile, not just session presence. A predicate function receives the session profile and must return true to allow access. If it returns false the guard treats it as unauthorized and redirects with the denied URL recorded. See feature4_predicate_authorization.json and feature6_composed_authorization.json for examples of single and composed rules.

trigger_keywords: composed guards
stacking multiple auth rules
combining two authorization layers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Multiple guard layers can be stacked on a single route. ALL layers must independently authorize the session before protected content renders. If any layer rejects, the redirect fires with the denied URL. The redirect search state persists across subsequent session-change events until full authorization is granted. See feature6_composed_authorization.json.

trigger_keywords: props passed to protected component
what does the guarded component receive
route context and session forwarded to child
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When authorization succeeds, the protected component receives: (1) router props including the current route path, (2) selected session data fields (e.g., email, first_name, last_name from the auth selector), and (3) any props supplied by the parent component (e.g., a boolean flag). All three categories must be forwarded. See feature8_protected_component_props.json for exact field names expected.

trigger_keywords: static metadata on wrapped component
preserving component properties after wrapping
static functions available on guarded component
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any static properties or callable fields attached to the original protected component must remain accessible on the wrapper component returned by the guard. This includes static boolean flags and static functions that must remain callable and return the same results. See feature9_component_metadata.json.

trigger_keywords: nested route full URL preserved
parent child route redirect path
nested path in redirect query
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For nested routes (e.g., `/parent/child`), the FULL nested URL including the child segment must be encoded in the `redirect` query parameter when redirecting. It must not be truncated to just the parent path. On logout from a nested route, the same full nested path is re-encoded in the redirect. See feature7_nested_routes.json.

trigger_keywords: duplicate redirects after mount
redirect fires more than once
prevent repeated redirect calls
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The guard must not fire duplicate redirect actions after the component mounts if the authorization state has not changed. Specifically: after initial mount the redirect fires exactly once, and after a subsequent auth state change that does not alter the authorization outcome the redirect count must still be exactly 1 (not incremented again). See feature13_redirect_mechanisms.json, `single_redirect` flow.

trigger_keywords: route parameter in authorization
using URL params in access rule
dynamic route segments in policy
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Authorization predicates can receive route parameters (e.g., `/ownProps/:id`) alongside session data. The policy can compare a session field against a route param value to allow or deny access. For example, a user with id=1 is allowed on `/ownProps/1` but denied on `/ownProps/2`. See feature11_route_parameter_policy.json.

trigger_keywords: dynamic failure destination
redirect target based on route params
failure path computed from state
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The failure destination (redirect target) can be a function rather than a static string. It receives current state and route parameters and returns the destination path dynamically. For example, `/login/1` vs `/login/0` depending on the route param. The denied URL is still appended as the `redirect` query parameter. See feature12_dynamic_failure_destination.json.

trigger_keywords: entry guard before route activation
onEnter guard hook
pre-activation route protection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In addition to a component-level wrapper, there is a route-entry guard that can be used before a route activates (e.g., an `onEnter` hook). It performs the same redirect with the same return-location query encoding. The attempted path `/onEnter` is encoded as `?redirect=%2FonEnter` in the redirect. See feature10_entry_guard.json.

trigger_keywords: redirect mechanism without dispatch
context router redirect fallback
redirect without explicit action
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Redirects can work through the router context directly when no explicit dispatch action is supplied in the configuration. The redirect behavior (path and search encoding) remains identical to the dispatch-based approach. See feature13_redirect_mechanisms.json, `context_router_redirect` policy.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The session selector and auth state shape used in previous auth flows — specifically how the `authSelector` extracts session data and how the `authenticatingSelector` signals a pending state. Relevant to how `start_authentication`, `login`, and `logout` events map to internal session state in the test harness policies (e.g., `requires_session`, `pending_custom_loading`).

The known bug of duplicate redirect dispatches firing after component mount when authorization state has not changed — addressed in feature13_redirect_mechanisms.json under the `single_redirect` flow, which asserts `redirect_calls_after_mount=1` and `redirect_calls_after_auth_change=1`.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the exact technical details on hand. I'd suggest looking at the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` — the expected outputs there are pretty explicit about what the behavior should be. If you can tell me which specific scenario you're running into I can try to point you to the right test case.

feature10_entry_guard.json.description: The route-entry guard can be used before route activation and redirects unauthorized navigation using the same return-location query behavior.

feature11_route_parameter_policy.json.description: Authorization decisions can combine selected session data with route parameters supplied by the router.

feature12_dynamic_failure_destination.js.description: The failure destination can be selected dynamically from current state and route parameters while still preserving the denied URL in the redirect query.

feature13_redirect_mechanisms.json.description: Redirects work through router context when no explicit dispatch action is supplied, and unchanged authorization state does not trigger duplicate redirect actions after mount.

feature1_redirect_unauthenticated.json.description: A protected route redirects an unauthenticated visitor to the login route and records the attempted URL in a query parameter.

feature2_pending_authentication.json.description: While authentication is still pending, the guarded route remains active and renders either the configured loading view or the default empty placeholder instead of redirecting.

cases.expected_output:
step=0
event=visit:/alwaysAuthDef
path=/alwaysAuthDef
search=
rendered=empty
placeholder_props={}

feature9_component_metadata.json.description: Metadata and callable fields attached to a protected component remain available on the guarded component returned by the wrapper.
